anti_pattern_rules: Keyword extraction is forbidden in any form: extracting words from text A, searching for them in text B, and drawing any conclusion from matches. See PM-0001, PM-0002.
Scripts present information for human review. Scripts never assign verdicts based on mechanical text matching.
No automated status assignment (CLEAR, FLAGGED, NEEDS_REVIEW, KEEP, SKIP) based on text matching.

COVERED verdict anti-patterns — three forbidden shortcuts: (1) Topic-level match: 'book argues X, finding argues X, therefore covered' is WRONG. Must name the specific evidence the finding brings AND where the book already presents that same specific evidence. (2) 'Same argument' without evidence comparison: 'ch1 makes this exact argument' is not enough. Does the book already present this specific scholar's framing, this specific quote, this specific example? If any of these are new, verdict is new_evidence_existing_argument, not covered. (3) Implicit coverage: 'the concept is implicit in the framework' or 'this is a variant of what is covered' means the book does NOT state it — therefore NOT covered. Any of these prevent a covered verdict: a named concept the book doesn't use, a specific mechanism the book doesn't describe, a new scope claim, or a new specific source text (e.g., book has Mark-Homer mimesis but finding has Mark-Aeneid — different source, not a variant). Test: does the inventory list this exact item? If you must argue it is 'implied by' or 'a variant of' an existing item, it is NOT covered.

The verdict matrix has two axes — is the ARGUMENT new, and is the EVIDENCE new? covered: argument in book AND evidence in book (or Q&A addresses it). new_evidence_existing_argument: argument in book, evidence NOT in book — new evidence to bolster an existing argument. new_argument_existing_evidence: argument NOT in book, evidence IS in book — someone looked at the same source we cite and drew a different conclusion. Often the most valuable: data already verified, only the argument is new. Do NOT downgrade to new_evidence_existing_argument or covered just because the evidence appears in the book. new_evidence_and_argument: argument NOT in book, evidence NOT in book — both need evaluation.

findings.video_title: Two Hours with Barbara DeLong--The Historical Jesus
findings.description: Tabor establishes that James, Jesus's brother, served as the definitive institutional leader of the Jerusalem church, making final decisions on major theological matters. In Acts 15, when the question of admitting Gentiles arises, "James stood up and said I've listened to you both and my decision is," indicating a formal decision-making authority structure characteristic of the ekklesia model discussed in the book.
findings.quote: and then James stood up and said I've listened to you both and my decision is... and then at the end of Acts acts 21 Paul goes back to Jerusalem after all his travels and guess who he goes to see first thing he's in town and he went in and met with James because he's the leader

findings.video_title: Did Earliest Jewish-Christianity Influence Islam?
findings.description: Tabor's analysis of Paul's transformation of the original Jewish Christianity movement—from a kingdom-focused, Torah-observant community into a gentile church centered on salvation through divine Jesus—supports the book's argument about institutional transformation and the ekklesia's evolution from Jewish to Greco-Christian frameworks.
findings.quote: the proclaimer became the one proclaimed Jesus preached the kingdom and it was the church that came and these are discontinuities that really lay uh expose uh the realities of the of Christianity

findings.video_title: If You Want to Follow Jesus: Go To James the Just
findings.description: Tabor demonstrates that Paul explicitly recognized James's superior authority as the first-named of the "three pillars," establishing a formal order of institutional authority that Paul deferred to in governance decisions.
findings.quote: 14 years after his conversion very close to ad50 he made a return trip to Jerusalem to receive authorization for his mission to the Gentiles from those he designated as the three pillars of the movement, namely James Peter and John... that he is named first by Paul Before Peter and John is absolutely critical... The order of the names indicates an established order of authority

findings.video_title: Die Apokalypse des Johannes (Teil 5): Die Lebenswelt einer antiken Großstadt (Ephesus) | 12.7.3
findings.description: The speaker emphasizes that the term *ekklesia* (Greek popular assembly of a polis) was deliberately adopted by early Christian communities, describing Christian communities as "a new kind of polis" where shared responsibility and a new form of public participation replaced the secular polis structure.
findings.quote: diese Volksversammlung erklärsia hieß ausgerechnet dieses Wort clesia oder eglisia besser wurde jetzt im Neuen Testament verwendet für die Bezeichnung der frühen christlichen Gemeinden... eine christliche Gemeinde ist eine neue Art von Police in der man gemeinschaftlich Verantwortung trägt und in der eine neue Art von Öffentlichkeit hergestellt wird

findings.video_title: Die Apokalypse des Johannes (Teil 5): Die Lebenswelt einer antiken Großstadt (Ephesus) | 12.7.3
findings.description: The speaker demonstrates how the concept of *polis* and its political-civic structure directly framed early Christian self-understanding, noting that Paul himself used political language (*politolmar* = citizenship) to describe the Christian community.
findings.quote: paulus schreibt einmal unsere Bürgerschaft politolmar ist im Himmel und damit will Paulus ausdrücken es geht um eine neue andere Art von Police in der andere Werte gelten und gelebt werden als in der säkularen Polis

findings.video_title: Die Apokalypse des Johannes (Teil 5): Die Lebenswelt einer antiken Großstadt (Ephesus) | 12.7.3
findings.description: The speaker confirms that most New Testament letters were addressed to or written in major Greek cities (Rome, Corinth, Philippi, Thessalonica, Ephesus, Colossae), establishing the geographic basis for understanding early Christianity within Hellenistic urban institutional networks.
findings.quote: die Mehrzahl der neutestamentlichen Briefe an eine großstadtgemeinde gerichtet waren oder in einer Großstadt verfasst worden sind man denke an Rom an Korinth an Philippi an Thessaloniki Ephesus und kolossé

findings.video_title: Das Markusevangelium | 9.3.1
findings.description: Mark frames the gospel as explicitly universal, to be preached to "alle nationen" (all nations), with deliberate narrative movements through Hellenistic territories integrating Gentiles into the messianic community—consistent with the book's thesis on ekklesia as Greek political institution.
findings.quote: markus füllt den begriff des evangeliums ganz klar universal markus 13 10 und 14 9 das evangelium soll alle nationen beziehungsweise in aller welt gepredigt werden

findings.video_title: Der 1. Thessalonicherbrief | 10.3.1
findings.description: The lecture confirms the Greek institutional terminology "ekklesia" for the Christian assembly and discusses its use in the Thessalonian context, supporting the book's argument that early Christianity adopted Greek political-institutional language.
findings.quote: das erste was wir erfahren und das ist schon mal ganz spannend ist einfach in diesem begriff der gemeinde ecclesia im griechischen gemeinde enthalten was war denn so eine gemeinde in der zeit des paulus das war natürlich so etwas wie eine kleine gruppe die sich in einem haus versammeln konnte

findings.video_title: Der 1. Thessalonicherbrief | 10.3.1
findings.description: The lecture explicitly compares the Parousia (Christ's return) to Roman imperial ceremonies and political staging ("politische Inszenierung"), indicating early Christian concepts adopted Greco-Roman political language and frameworks for understanding Christ's authority.
findings.quote: in der griechisch römischen welt auch diesen begriff borussia der bezieht sich da zum teil auf die ankunft eines feldherrn oder sogar eines kaisers... und das ist ein riesen inszenierung... es ist nichts anderes als eine politische Inszenierung

findings.video_title: »Durch Gottes Gnade bin ich, was ich bin«: Paulus – Heidenmissionar von Syrien bis Rom | 14.5.1
findings.description: The lecturer confirms that Paul strategically established Christian communities in major Hellenistic cities—Antioch (third-largest city of the Roman Empire with hundreds of thousands of inhabitants), Corinth, Ephesus, Philippi, and Thessalonica—demonstrating how the ekklesia spread through Greek urban centers as the book argues.
findings.quote: Antiochia war nach damaligen Verhältnissen eine Großstadt es war sogar die drittgrößte Stadt des römischen Reiches... man könnte sagen eben dass sie durch die vielen Einwanderer eine Multikulti Stadt war

findings.video_title: »Durch Gottes Gnade bin ich, was ich bin«: Paulus – Heidenmissionar von Syrien bis Rom | 14.5.1
findings.description: Paul's deliberate missionary strategy targeted "God-fearers" (Gottesfürchtige)—non-Jewish polytheists attracted to monotheism—showing how Christian institutional formation adapted specifically to Greek cultural contexts where monotheistic political assemblies could take root.
findings.quote: warum hat Paulus jetzt so ein großes Interesse gerade diese gottesfürchtigen anzusprechen weil das Menschen sind die bereits offen für das Judentum sind und damit offen und interessiert für eine monotheistische Religion

findings.video_title: »Durch Gottes Gnade bin ich, was ich bin«: Paulus – Heidenmissionar von Syrien bis Rom | 14.5.1
findings.description: The transcript describes Paul teaching in the "school of Tyrannus," explicitly employing Greco-Roman rhetorical forms for Christian proclamation—a concrete institutional parallel showing how early Christian ekklesia adopted Greek political-educational frameworks, filling the THIN area of "specific institutional parallels."
findings.quote: Paulus in der Schule des tyranos lehrafträge hielt das heißt die Kommunikationsform antiker retoren für die verit der christlichen Botschaft nutzte

findings.video_title: »Durch Gottes Gnade bin ich, was ich bin«: Paulus – Heidenmissionar von Syrien bis Rom | 14.5.1
findings.description: Paul's missionary foundation work was conducted through a collegial team structure with multiple named associates (Silas, Timothy, Titus, Priscilla, Aquila) serving as co-authors and sharing authority, mirroring Greek political assembly governance models rather than hierarchical models.
findings.quote: Paulus geht bei seinen Gemeindegründungen auch sehr kollegial vor das heißt Teamarbeit

findings.video_title: »Ein Eiferer für Gott« (Apg 22,3): Paulus – vom Christenverfolger zum Heidenmissionar | 14.4.2
findings.description: Paul's Roman citizenship protected him from torture in legal proceedings, and his appeal to Caesar exemplifies concrete institutional access within Roman colonial administration that enabled his mobility and missionary work across Hellenistic cities.
findings.quote: römische Bürger durften nicht gefoltert werden... am Ende appelliert Paulus ja sogar an den Kaiser das heißt dass sein Prozess vor dem Kaiser fortgeführt werden solle und das war dann ja auch der Grund warum er nach Rom übergeführt wird

findings.video_title: Die Briefe des Paulus | 14.7.1
findings.description: The lecture documents Koine Greek as the institutional language of Eastern provinces, including in Roman colonial administration, providing evidence that early Christian communication operated within an established Greek institutional framework.
findings.quote: In den östlichen Provinzen wurde durchwegs griechisch verwendet. Nicht nur im Alltag von der Bevölkerung, sondern sogar in der römischen Administration, in der römischen Verwaltung. Der einzige Bereich, der Latein war, war der Bereich der Armee.

findings.video_title: Die Briefe des Paulus | 14.7.1
findings.description: Paul's mastery of Greek letter-writing conventions demonstrates his institutional competence within Greek cultural frameworks, reinforcing the thesis that early Christian communication was fundamentally structured through Greek institutional forms.
findings.quote: Paulus war ein Meister des antiken Briefschreibens. der kennt sich aus, der weiß, welche Formeln man da verwendet, um bestimmte Wirkung zu erzielen. Der verwendet jene Formeln, die auch die Leserinnen und Leser gewohnt waren.

findings.video_title: Paulus und die Stoa – Warum ein stoischer Intellektueller im 1. Jh. Christ werden konnte | 14.4.1
findings.description: Early Christian communities functioned as alternative ethical and social institutions within Hellenistic cities, attracting educated individuals by transcending traditional social hierarchies (Jew/Greek, rich/poor, male/female). This institutional novelty and social appeal parallels Greek political-philosophical communities.
findings.quote: das frühe Christentum war eine bildungsreligion... das Leben in den frühchristlichen Gemeinden wo eine Anziehungskraft eine Faszination ausging... da können Sklaven reinkommen... alle sind eins in Christus

findings.video_title: Das römische Reich – die kulturelle und gesellschaftliche Heimat von Paulus von Tarsus | 14.6.1
findings.description: The lecture details institutional parallels between ancient Roman trade guilds (Vereine) and early Christian communities, demonstrating how Paul applied Greco-Roman civic structures to organize Christian assemblies. Both had officers (presidents/prostates, treasurers), regular meal assemblies with designated seating, membership contributions, moral codes, and mutual financial support systems.
findings.quote: Man braucht Funktionäre, man braucht jemand, der für die Finanzen zuständig ist, man braucht einen Gemeindeleiter. Das hat nichts mit Hierarchie zu tun noch bei Paulus. Das ist nichts sakrales, das ist was ganz ziviles...die bekommen die kommen aus dem zivilen Bereich der römischen Gesellschaft.

findings.video_title: Das römische Reich – die kulturelle und gesellschaftliche Heimat von Paulus von Tarsus | 14.6.1
findings.description: Paul's status as a Roman citizen and skilled weaver from Tarsus granted him institutional access to Greco-Roman trade networks and enabled him to establish Christian communities in these same civic spaces through his craft (weaving/tentmaking). The lecture emphasizes Paul deliberately used his professional and civic knowledge to structure early churches, borrowing terminology and organizational models from guild apprenticeships and civic associations.
findings.quote: Die Vereine sind etwas ausgezeichnetes, um die mitzudenken, wenn man an frühkristliche Gemeinden denkt...It's good to think with.

findings.video_title: Der Römerbrief – Teil 2 | 10.8.3
findings.description: The lecture demonstrates how Paul strategically employs politically charged Greek terminology to construct an alternative community. "Pistis" (faith/loyalty) corresponds to Roman "fides," the institutional bond holding the empire together through patronage networks and social obligation. By redirecting pistis toward Christ, Paul creates a community that is "necessarily political" (zwangsläufig politisch), necessarily set apart from imperial structures—evidence of how ekklesia functions as an alternative political assembly organized through reoriented loyalty.
findings.quote: piste ist lateinisch vieles ist das was das imperium im innersten zusammenhält... alle abhängigkeit sanitäts verbindungen werden durch die fidesz oder piste ist bestimmt... der christus glaube ist aber auch der baum dream anker eine gemeinschaft die... setzt sich von der umgebenden kultur ab und wird zwangsläufig politisch

chapter_inventory.argument: The early Christian movement is best understood as a continuation of the Greek imperial tradition, not a new religion started from scratch.
evidence: Jesus framed in Greek and Egyptian royal cult from previous chapters
Christianity appears from the beginning as an extension of Greek imperial tradition
Jesus inherited a movement spanning a major part of Rome
Matthew 28:19 Great Commission: poreuthentes oun matheteusate panta ta ethne

chapter_inventory.argument: 'Panta ta ethne' (all the nations) refers to the nations of the Greek world (the oikoumene), not to all peoples universally or to non-Jews.
evidence: Hebrew goyim does not carry the same meaning in Hellenistic Greek usage
Hellenistic inscriptions use 'panta ta ethne' for the oikoumene, the civilized world subject to the emperor
Priene calendar inscription (9 BC) describes Augustus's birthday as 'gospel for all the nations' referring to imperial subjects
Gen 22:18 LXX quoted in Galatians 3:8: nations folded into God's promise, not outsiders
Ps 148:11,14 LXX: kings and peoples of the earth under God's rule with Israel as center

chapter_inventory.argument: Acts of the Apostles makes more sense as a record of maintaining Greek civic order under Roman rule than as a history of religious missionary expansion.
evidence: Churches in Acts appear fully formed with no indication of how they were established
Very little religious conversion described in Acts
Paul's speech in Athens about the 'unknown god' exploits existing Greek 'highest god' discourse
Speeches in Acts are political, not missionary in character
In Ephesus Paul attacks physical idols of Artemis directly rather than finding commonalities
Apostolos was a term for emissaries of centralized state for centuries
Ekklesia was the civic assembly of the city, local government
Well-developed councils, bishops, deacons, and treasuries described immediately
References to letters of recommendation, common law between ecclesiae, newly appointed officials
chapter_inventory.section: Are Acts a work of fiction? (lines 36-63)

chapter_inventory.argument: The pattern of Paul writing to so many churches in such a short period is incompatible with newly founded congregations but consistent with pre-existing Greek civic assemblies.
evidence: Churches appear immediately in every major city of the former Greek empire
No churches appear anywhere outside the former Greek empire
All correspondence and scripture written exclusively in Greek
Greek was not lingua franca in Roman territories outside the former Greek empire; Latin was
No epistles to major non-Greek cities: Mediolanum, Lutetia, Aquilea, Lugdunum, Memphis, Londinium
Total absence of Aramaic or Hebrew letters
All letters are to Greek assemblies in Greek

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's letters function as imperial chancery correspondence to civic assemblies, not as private devotional notes.
evidence: Paul addresses ekklesiai, the same civic assemblies that governed Greek cities
He orders public reading, courier circulation, and inter-city dissemination matching administrative circulars
Greetings from co-workers function like official co-signatories
Directives on discipline, finance, and adjudication mirror instructions from higher authority to subordinate poleis
chapter_inventory.section: Paul as Imperial Envoy and Administrator (lines 80-86)

chapter_inventory.argument: The division between 'authentic' and 'deutero-Pauline' letters rests on an anachronistic definition of authorship; ancient delegated composition was standard practice.
evidence: Romans 16:22 names Tertius as the physical writer, separating authority from penmanship
Paul's autograph formulas are ratification marks: 1 Cor 16:21, Gal 6:11, Col 4:18, 2 Thess 3:17
Greek and Roman elite figures (strategoi, envoys, league officials) issued instructions orally and entrusted composition to staff
Administrative model predicts stylistic variation across authorized letters
Named associates carry letters, manage funds, arbitrate disputes, represent Paul in person

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's political profile matches that of an authorized representative of the Christ-emperor to the Greek cities.
evidence: Paul appeals to Caesar as a Roman citizen
He travels through the network of Greek poleis that formed Alexander's empire
Athens speech (Acts 17) is a philosophical-diplomatic speech pitched at elites
Acts 16: Paul and Silas sing in prison until earthquake opens doors, echoing Euripides' Bacchae (imprisoned Dionysus)
Acts 17: Paul debates in the Areopagus like Socrates before the Athenian elite

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's vocabulary is drawn from court hierarchy and administrative register, not devotional language.
evidence: Thronoi (sovereign seats of rule) in Col 1:16, Eph 1:21
Kyriotetes (lordships with jurisdictional dominion) in Col 1:16, Eph 1:21
Archai (magistracies and command offices) in Col 1:16, Eph 1:21
Exousiai (delegated legal powers) in Col 1:16, Eph 1:21
Christ enthroned 'at the right hand' above every office (Eph 1:20-21)

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's military language is constitutional and administrative, not merely metaphorical; it belongs to a full register of allegiance and governance.
evidence: 'Soldier of Christ Jesus' as enlistment into imperial service (2 Tim 2:3)
'Armor of God' equips troops of the king (Eph 6:10-17)
'I bear the stigmata of Jesus' marks the branded body of a sworn man (Gal 6:17)
'Sealed with the Spirit' as bureaucratic seal of a new jurisdiction (Eph 1:13)
'Jesus is Lord' as oath of allegiance (Rom 10:9)
Stoic writers use soldier-language for private discipline but never combine it with branding, sealing, thrones, officers, and assemblies

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's civic vocabulary explicitly maps believers into a political order under Christ as supreme magistrate.
evidence: 'Our politeuma is in heaven' declares citizenship in a capital city with its own law (Phil 3:20)
Episkopoi and diakonoi are administrative offices (Phil 1:1)
'God has appointed, first apostles, second prophets, third teachers' is rank ordering (1 Cor 12:28)
'The saints will judge the world' grants appellate competence (1 Cor 6:2-3)
Greetings from 'those of Caesar's household' (Phil 4:22)
Praetorian Guard aware of his case (Phil 1:13)
Associate Epaphroditus shares the name of Nero's powerful secretary

chapter_inventory.argument: Ekklesia was not a new religious club but the same civic assembly that governed the Greek city before Roman conquest, now recognizing Christ instead of Rome.
evidence: Ekklesia was the sovereign body of citizens that passed laws, heard proclamations, received official dispatches
After conquest, populations kept assemblies, procedures, and language; only allegiance changed
All correspondence in Greek to historic poleis of the Greek world
Acts 6:5 lists the Seven with all Greek names: Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, Nicolas of Antioch
Nicolas identified as convert from Antioch, a major Greek metropolis

chapter_inventory.argument: Diakonoi and episkopoi were pre-existing civic offices, not Christian inventions.
evidence: Philippians 1:1 (c. 60-62 AD) addresses episkopoi and diakonoi as standard civic salutation
Romans 16:1 (c. 57 AD) commends Phoebe as diakonos of the assembly at Cenchreae
Erythrai decree (AIO 296, 5th century BC) names episkopoi as civic magistrates who 'allot and install the Council'
Statue base on Athenian Acropolis (IG II^2 3464) honours Syeris, diakonos of priestess Lysimache in cult of Athena Polias
Jewish diaspora inscriptions from Asia Minor list gerousiarch, grammateus, and diakonos together as standard communal offices
Inscriptions from Termessos and Mylasa attest ekklesia kyria still active under Roman rule in 1st century
Messene: classical assembly hall remodeled by episkopos Theodoulos in 4th century with mosaic floor inscription
Pastoral Epistles (1 Tim 3:1-13) codify qualifications for existing offices, not invent new ones
Diakonoi handle financial collections (2 Cor 8-9), coordinate logistics, serve at communal meals (1 Cor 11)
chapter_inventory.section: Diakonoi and episkopoi: civic offices already functioning (lines 169-190)

chapter_inventory.argument: The epistles employ the full vocabulary and procedures of Greek civic administration.
evidence: Politeuma (Phil 3:20) is civic enrollment in a political community with jurisdiction
Archai and exousiai (Rom 13:1, Col 1:16) are magistracies and jurisdictions from civic decrees
Romans 13 commands submission to Roman governing authorities during Jewish unrest
1 Cor 16:1-4: organized collection, appointed delegates, fund transfer to Jerusalem
2 Cor 8-9: inter-city financial coordination, accountability measures, competitive giving
Treasuries, ledgers, and auditing procedures presumed
1 Thess 5:27: letter charged to be read to all brothers (civic circular protocol)
Col 4:16: letter circulation between assemblies of different cities (inter-city decree exchange)
chapter_inventory.section: Civic governance procedures in the epistles (lines 191-216)

chapter_inventory.argument: The sociological behavior described in the epistles (status display, factions, economic competition) is inherited directly from the Greek civic assembly.
evidence: Aristophanes, Demosthenes, Athenian scholia describe 'overdressed youths' and status display at civic gatherings
1 Cor 11:17-34: rich arrive early, consume food/wine, leave poor with nothing at communal meal
1 Cor 11:2-16: debate over head coverings and appearance (civic stage presentation)
James 2:1-7: favoritism toward man with gold ring and fine clothes in assembly
Gold rings and reserved seating by status are Greek civic markers
1st-century diaspora synagogues adopted Greek governance structures (archons, gerousia, grammateis)
1 Cor 1:10-17: factions ('I follow Paul/Apollos/Cephas') mirror Greek civic patronage-line faction politics
chapter_inventory.section: Sociological continuity: status, factions, and the civic catwalk (lines 217-246)

chapter_inventory.argument: The institutional complexity of Christian assemblies appears fully formed within 20-25 years of Jesus' death, proving continuation rather than invention.
evidence: 1 Corinthians (c. 54-55 AD) already describes: multiple factions, established liturgy, complex financial systems, defined offices, discipline procedures, legal arbitration (1 Cor 6:1-8), public reading/circulation of letters
References to house meetings appear in later epistles (Rom 16:5, Phlm 1:2, Col 4:15), suggesting shift to covert locations under Roman surveillance
Next Christian city after NT Greek world is Carthage (c. 180-200 AD, Scillitan Martyrs and Tertullian)
Carthage appears 120-140 years later despite being a major connected port city

chapter_inventory.argument: The geographic distribution of early Christianity perfectly matches the former Alexandrian imperial civic network, proving political rather than missionary origins.
evidence: 1 Clement (c. 96 AD), Ignatius (c. 110 AD), Didache (c. 80-120 AD), Shepherd of Hermas (c. 90-140 AD), Papias (c. 120 AD), Quadratus (c. 125 AD), Aristides (c. 125-135 AD), Polycarp (mid-2nd c.), Hegesippus (c. 160-180 AD) — none mention any Western location
Clement of Rome and Shepherd of Hermas written in Rome mention no Western Christian communities beyond Rome itself
Every major Greek-speaking city in Alexander's successor kingdoms appears in early Christian sources
Greek cities outside Alexandrian imperial lattice (Massalia, Sicily, northern Black Sea colonies) do not appear despite shared language, commerce, and literacy
Preservation bias cannot reproduce a geopolitical frontier pattern
Trade diffusion predicts early penetration of Massalia/Sicily (not observed)
Missionary diffusion predicts coastal bleed-over (not observed)
Linguistic diffusion predicts Greek-on-Greek transmission (not observed)
Every major Greek-speaking city has a Christian assembly by 100 AD without exception
Not one non-Greek city shows a church until more than a century later

chapter_inventory.argument: The early assemblies were hybrid institutions where civic assembly, diaspora synagogue, and voluntary association logics overlap, but civic practice dominates.
evidence: Diaspora synagogues functioned with civic-administrative features: officers, treasurers, communal courts
Assemblies elect officers, collect funds, adjudicate disputes, send delegates (citing Park, Esler, van Kooten)
Tiberius Julius Alexander: Greek-educated Jewish aristocrat, procurator of Judea (44-46 AD), commanded alongside Titus at siege of Jerusalem
Julia Severa in Phrygian Acmonia: simultaneously priestess of Imperial cult and benefactor of local synagogue

chapter_inventory.argument: Papyrological evidence confirms Roman administration treated the ekklesia as a corporate body with officers, treasurers, buildings, and legal presence.
evidence: P.Oxy. 8.1138: money-tax paid 'on behalf of the ekklesia' in bureaucratic formula used for professional guilds
P.Oxy. 33.2673 (Great Persecution): inventories church property with standard confiscation language, listing books, vessels, assets

chapter_inventory.argument: Exhaustive mapping of every city in Acts and Epistles reveals alignment with former Greek empire territories, not random missionary expansion.
evidence: Complete table of cities in Acts: Jerusalem, Bethany, Joppa, Caesarea, Antioch, Nazareth, Tarsus, Lystra, Derbe, Iconium, Ephesus, Miletus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea, Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea, Athens, Corinth, Neapolis, Alexandria, Cyrene, Puteoli, Rome, Cyprus, Salamis, Paphos, Patara, Tyre

Complete table of cities in Epistles: Rome, Corinth, Ephesus, Galatia, Philippi, Thessalonica, Colossae, Laodicea, Crete, Cyprus, Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, Bithynia, Macedonia, Miletus, Antioch, Tarsus, Syria, Asia, Troas, Berea, Paphos, Puteoli
Map visualization showing correlation with borders of Eastern Roman Empire
Trip to Rome was substantially different in nature to other trips

chapter_inventory.argument: Opposition to Christianity in Greek cities was framed as civic/economic disorder, not theological rejection, consistent with Christianity as continuation of Greek imperial philosophy.
evidence: Ephesian silversmiths riot over lost business
Thessalonian authorities worry about sedition
Philippian magistrates object to public disturbance
Only organized ideological opposition comes through Judean leadership networks
Colonial coins in Syria during Paul's lifetime bore Jupiter, Aries, crescent moon as emblems of imperial rule
Ekklesiai redirected existing imperial vocabulary toward Christ as sovereign
chapter_inventory.section: Minimal resistance to the acceptance of the new religion (lines 450-459)

chapter_inventory.argument: Early Christians used secret symbols and conspiratorial methods consistent with an underground political movement, not a public religious club.
evidence: Secret symbols: fish, anchor, staurogram (tau-rho combining Greek letters for cross)
Night meetings, communal meals, coded iconography
Clement of Alexandria names fish and anchor as Christian seals and traces anchor to Seleucid royal usage
Tau-rho replicates Egyptian ankh shape (vertical stem, horizontal bar, loop at top)
Tau-rho appears in oldest surviving Christian manuscripts: P45, P66, P75, all from Egypt
Socrates Scholasticus reports cross-like signs at Serapeum (391) read by Egyptians as 'life to come' (HE 5.17)
Chi-rho appears on Christian gems as early as mid-3rd century before Constantine
Both Mithras and Christianity operated as discreet, oath-bound, initiatory brotherhoods
Pauline language of 'soldiers of Christ' fits conspiratorial military model
chapter_inventory.section: Even though the religion was so successful... (lines 460-474)

chapter_inventory.argument: Christians were persecuted not for theological reasons but for refusing imperial cult while insisting Christ was king, yet the standard Nero persecution narrative has serious historiographical problems.
evidence: Tacitus passage (Annals 15.44) contains variant 'Chrestianos' not 'Christianos'
Tacitus passage appears nowhere else in ancient literature for centuries
Tacitus writing 50 years after the events seems unfamiliar with the group
Pliny the Younger (Letters 10.96) indicates he never participated in trials of Christians and was unsure of procedure
Peter executed in Rome by crucifixion (reserved for crimes against the state)
Paul executed by beheading (as Roman citizen, for same crimes)
Crucifixion reserved for sedition and treason
chapter_inventory.section: Christianity was uniquely legible to Roman authorities (lines 476-489)

chapter_inventory.argument: 'Soldiers of Christ' language was taken literally by Roman authorities as designating an oath-bound loyalty group organized like an army.
evidence: Roman writers used soldier-language for oath-bound groups with secret loyalties
Cicero describes Catiline's civilian followers as an exercitus (Catiline Orations 2.5)
Josephus describes followers of Judas the Galilean with 'immovable resolution' (Antiquities 18.23)
Plutarch describes political factions as acting 'like soldiers' (Life of Sertorius 26)
Paul: stratiotes Christou (2 Tim 2:3), systratiotes (Phil 2:25, Phlm 2), strateia (1 Cor 9:7, 2 Cor 10:3-4), panoplia (Eph 6:11)
Pliny, Tacitus, Acta Scillitanorum accuse Christians of illicit meetings, secret oaths, refusing imperial cult, collegium illicitum
Paul's cities (Philippi, Corinth, Thessalonica, Ephesus, Antioch) all had permanent or semi-permanent Roman garrisons

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's focus on restoring the kingdom of God through resurrecting the office of the Christos explains his silence on Jesus's daily sayings and teachings.
evidence: Shocking lack of references to teachings of Jesus, Jewish law, events of Jesus's life and death in Paul's letters
Paul's religion is about resurrecting the office of Christos, the rightful king
The idea of God sending a king to restore the Greek empire headed by Christos
chapter_inventory.section: Paul barely mentions the life of Jesus (lines 519-527)

chapter_inventory.argument: The absence of Alexandria in the New Testament is best explained by Alexandria being the center of the movement (point of origin), not a mission field.
evidence: Alexandria hosted the largest Jewish community outside Palestine (Philo estimates 1 million in In Flaccum 43)
Alexandria produced the Septuagint, Wisdom literature, Philo's philosophical corpus, vocabulary of Gospel of John
No epistle addresses Alexandria; no mission narrative targets it
Pattern: apostles write from metropolitan centers to mission fields (James not to Jerusalem, Peter not to Antioch)
Apollos arrives from Alexandria already trained in Scriptures (Acts 18:24), needing only correction on baptism
Old Testament largely omits Ptolemaic empire despite its centrality; NT similarly minimizes Alexandria

chapter_inventory.argument: Acts of the Apostles is best classified as pragmatic Greco-Roman historiography concerned with power, legitimacy, and regime formation, not as fiction.
evidence: Precise civic offices confirmed by inscriptions: politarchs in Thessalonica, Asiarchs in Ephesus, proconsul in Cyprus
Legal procedures, assembly competences, travel routes, ports, intercity logistics with administrative precision
'We' passages function as itinerary notes, not literary devices
Treasuries, letters of recommendation, jurisdictional disputes, debates over common law between assemblies
Luke's preface (Luke 1:1-4) follows formal historiographic conventions
Comparison with Polybius who writes history to explain how sovereignties rise and stabilize
Basileia denotes real sovereignty and jurisdiction throughout Acts
Acts 1:6 frames narrative as restoration of a kingdom
Final scene: Paul in Rome proclaiming 'the kingdom of God' as regime proclamation

chapter_inventory.argument: Key episodes in Acts follow patterns of royal enthronement, imperial apotheosis, and political inauguration.
evidence: Acts 1:6: 'Lord, will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?' implies political kingship claim
Acts 1:9-11: Ascension mimics apotheosis scenes (Alexander, Roman emperors)
Acts 2: Pentecost multilingual miracle and mass conversion reflects imperial ideal of uniting nations under one divine king
Acts 5:34-39: Gamaliel references Theudas and Judas the Galilean, acknowledging political nature of messianic revolts
Acts 7:2-53: Stephen's anti-Temple speech echoes Philo (De Cherubim 99-101) and Stoic criticisms of Temple literalism
Acts ends in Rome with Paul preaching 'the kingdom of God' — no narrative climax because the message is the empire is now Christian
chapter_inventory.section: Acts opens with royal enthronement / various Acts subsections (lines 566-604)

chapter_inventory.argument: James the Just wrote within the same Hellenistic tradition, with polished literary Koine and moral-philosophical register, functioning as the dynastic successor to Jesus.
evidence: James was brother of Jesus, next in line to the throne in dynastic framework
Honorific 'the Just' functions within Hellenistic titulature system as Soter or Epiphanes
Rhetorical style matches Hellenistic philosophical moral teaching (diatribe with imaginary interlocutors, imperatival exhortation)
James 1:21 Greek text with emphuton logon ('implanted word')
Over sixty words unique in the New Testament
Rhetorical techniques: paronomasia, alliteration, personification, vivid rhetorical questions
Level of Greek difficult to reconcile with illiterate village laborer

chapter_inventory.argument: All three standard models for the rise of Christianity (missionary diffusion, Judaic sect, imperial cult competition) fail to account for the observed data.
evidence: Missionary Diffusion Model (Harnack, Stark) predicts gradual spread, multilingual docs, continuous growth; observed: sudden empire-wide network exclusively in Greek, then century of stagnation

Judaic Sect Model (Eisenman, Sanders, Vermes) predicts Aramaic/Hebrew writings, Mosaic law reliance, synagogue-based diffusion; observed: no Aramaic/Hebrew letters, hostility to Mosaic law, universal Greek correspondence

Imperial Cult Competition Model (Friesen, Harland) predicts spread into Latin West, random scatter, syncretism; observed: no Latin West spread, perfect coverage of former Greek empire with uniform royal Christ claims
Zero presence, immediate imperial coverage, then long stagnation admits only one explanation: continuation of Greek empire

chapter_inventory.argument: The New Testament corpus maps onto four imperial poles, with patristic attestation uniformly supporting the four gospel attributions.
evidence: Papias (via Eusebius HE 3.39): Mark wrote as Peter's interpreter; Matthew compiled the logia
Irenaeus (Adv. Haer. 3.1.1): lists Matthew, Mark, Luke (Paul's companion), John
Muratorian Fragment (late 2nd c.): names Luke and John as 3rd and 4th gospels, recognizes Pauline corpus
Clement of Alexandria and Origen repeat these attributions
1 Clement (c. 96) cites 1 Corinthians
Ignatius (c. 110) presupposes Pauline churches and diction
Polycarp (c. 135) quotes multiple Pauline letters and 1 Peter
No early counter-attribution of the four gospels to other names
chapter_inventory.section: The Corpus of the New Testament / Patristic attestations (lines 657-678)

chapter_inventory.argument: Early papyri show natural clustering (Pauline, Petrine/Jude, Johannine) that mirrors the four-pole architecture.
evidence: P46 (c. 200): Pauline collection (Rom, 1-2 Cor, Gal, Eph/Col, Phil, 1 Thess, Heb)
P66 (c. 200) and P75 (early 3rd c.): major witnesses to John and Luke/John
P9 (3rd c.): 1 John at Oxyrhynchus
P72 (3rd/4th c.): 1-2 Peter and Jude
P13 (3rd c.): Hebrews
P45 (3rd c.): Gospels/Acts
Egyptian findspots reflect preservation bias but composition clustering tracks the imperial map

chapter_inventory.argument: The four canonical gospels correspond to four great post-Alexandrian cultural worlds: Ptolemaic Egypt, Seleucid Syria, Judean kingdom, and Antigonid Aegean.
evidence: Ptolemaic Egypt: Alexandria as center of scholarship, Jewish-Greek synthesis, Logos theology, Platonic dualisms — produced Gospel of John
Seleucid Syria: Antioch as Greek metropolis with Aramaic-speaking countryside, natural bilingualism — produced Gospel of Mark with Aramaic phrases
Judean kingdom: Hasmonean/Herodian dynasty with own law schools, dynastic memory — produced Gospel of Matthew with genealogy, legal-exegetical argument
Antigonid world: gymnasia, rhetoric schools, polis assemblies, historiographic tradition; Ephesus as de facto metropolis — produced Gospel of Luke with polished historiographic Greek
These four worlds persisted as cultural facts under Roman administration
chapter_inventory.section: The Four Surviving Greek Cultural Worlds After Alexander (lines 690-739)

chapter_inventory.argument: The fourfold structure of the gospels traces back to ancient Near Eastern royal symbols predating Alexander by over a millennium.
evidence: Ezekiel's four-faced creature (6th century BC): lion, bull, nesher, human
Hebrew nesher does not denote zoologically precise 'eagle' but royal bird of prey compatible with Horus falcon
Egypt: crowned falcon of Horus on every cartouche from Old Kingdom onward; on standards, palace facades, military banners
Ptolemies adopt Eagle of Zeus as Greek equivalent of Horus-falcon; becomes Roman legionary eagle, then European heraldic symbol
Phoenician/Aramaean world: bull as supreme symbol of Baal and Hadad; Ugaritic tablets (13th-12th century BC); bronze bulls at temple gates in Byblos, Tyre, Sidon
Golden calf in Bible = official Phoenician state god
Lion of Judah: state emblem attested in royal ideology since David, reinforced in Gen 49; lion imagery on Judean seals, storage jars
Assyrian lion-hunt reliefs; Babylon's lion avenues
Greek heroic human: Mycenaean frescoes, Geometric pottery hoplite ranks, kouros sculpture, armed citizen as civic emblem for 7+ centuries before Ezekiel
chapter_inventory.section: The Four Royal Symbols Before Alexander (lines 742-789)

chapter_inventory.argument: Irenaeus's explanation that four gospels correspond to four living creatures was not numerology but preserved a fragment of ancient fourfold political logic.
evidence: Irenaeus (Adv. Haer. 3.11.8): 'the living creatures are quadriform, and the Gospel is quadriform'
Revelation 4:6-8: four living creatures around the throne (lion, calf, human, eagle) chanting the Trisagion
Revelation's Beast = Nero (666 = Hebrew gematria of Neron Kaisar; 616 variant = Latin spelling)
Babylon = Rome (seven hills, rules over kings of the earth)
Origen (homilies on Ezekiel and Luke) ties four to rivers of Eden, winds, corners of earth
Victorinus of Pettau (earliest Revelation commentator): 'The four living creatures are the four Gospels'
Augustine (De Consensu Evangelistarum 1.6): four living creatures as figures of four evangelists
Jerome standardizes Western assignment: man=Matthew, lion=Mark, ox=Luke, eagle=John
All Church Fathers disagree on animal-evangelist pairings but agree absolutely on fourfoldness

chapter_inventory.argument: The four state emblems of the four Hellenistic successor cultures match Ezekiel's four creatures and the four gospels.
evidence: Ptolemaic Eagle of Zeus: central badge on every major coin type (silver, gold, bronze) standing on thunderbolt; on military standards, naval ensigns
Seleucid bull: Seleucus I Nicator's gold/silver showing Bucephalus with ox-horns (SC 1-9), inscription BASILEŌS SELEUKOU; subsequent coins SC 130-133, 379, 505-525, 841-856, 1420-1421
Hasmonean/Herodian lion: lion of Judah carved into fortress stones, stamped on royal seals; Ethiopia carried Lion of Judah on flag until 20th century; still emblem of Jerusalem
Antigonid human warrior: crested Corinthian helmet, hoplite profile as emblem of Macedonian/Greek polis
chapter_inventory.section: Why Irenaeus Was Not Wrong / State emblems (lines 848-898)

chapter_inventory.argument: Before the universal fourfold canon, each region had its own dominant scriptural tradition matching the four-pole model.
evidence: Oxyrhynchus earliest manuscripts overwhelmingly Johannine (P90, P5, P39, P106 all Gospel of John, c. 150-225)
P9 preserving 1 John (c. 175-225) confirms parallel circulation of Johannine epistolary material
Ratio of Johannine to Matthean text in early Egyptian papyri approximately 70:1
No early Oxyrhynchus papyrus contains Mark or a fourfold gospel collection
Luke appears only paired with John in P75, not as independent Egyptian tradition
Egypt uniquely preserves clustered non-canonical Johannine texts: Gospel of Thomas, Gospel of Philip, Gospel of Truth, Valentinian homilies, Apocryphon of John
P52 (Rylands fragment, John 18): conventionally dated c. 125-175; paleography permits late 1st-early 2nd century (Nongbri)
Papias commonly placed c. 120 but date drifts later from theological assumptions
Papias: Matthew composed 'in the Hebrew dialect' (distinct Judean tradition)
Papias identifies Mark as Peter's interpreter (Antiochene network)
Marcion (c. 140) circulated Aegean canon: edited Luke and Pauline Apostolikon
Irenaeus (c. 180) first to argue programmatically for exactly four gospels
P45 (c. 200-250) is earliest physical codex with all four gospels
chapter_inventory.section: The Regional Canon Before the Universal Canon (lines 900-931)

chapter_inventory.argument: The Gospel of John is the Alexandrian gospel, with Logos theology, Philo's philosophical vocabulary, and Egyptian ritual parallels.
evidence: John's opening Logos matches Philo's De Opificio Mundi 6-25 (Logos as divine rational structure ordering cosmos)
Monogenes (John 1:14, 1:18) mirrors Philo's use in De Sacrificiis 41
Charis and aletheia pairing (John 1:14) matches Philo Legum Allegoriae 3.207
Light-darkness dualism (phos/skotos) belongs to Alexandrian Middle Platonism (Philo, Quis Rerum Divinarum Heres 87-88)
Earliest material witnesses P66 and P75 from Egypt with Egyptian scribal spelling patterns
Egyptian temple ritual sequence (procession, elevation, revelation, adoration, recession) parallels Christian Mass
Eucharistic Adoration/Benediction sequence structurally identical to Egyptian temple processions
Corpus Christi processions extend pattern into public space
Egyptian monstrance-like solar disk imagery on shrines mirrors Christian monstrance sunburst form
Egyptian bread in temple economy: offered to god, receives divine force, redistributed to priests
John's 'living bread' discourse (John 6) closer to Egyptian divine sustenance than to Palestinian Judaism

chapter_inventory.argument: John's theology systematically parallels Egyptian theology: Amun as hidden Father, Ra as visible Son, Logos as mediator, and judgment/eternal life relocated to the present.
evidence: Amun as hidden source = John's God unseen/inaccessible (John 1:18, 5:37, 6:46)
Ra as visible manifestation giving life/light (Book of the Dead Ch. 15) = Son as light and life (John 1:4-9, 8:12, 12:46)
Ptah creates by word: heart's thought (Sia) and tongue's command (Hu) (Shabaka Stone, Memphite Theology)
Coffin Texts Spell 80: Atum governs by command and 'with this mouth of mine'; CT Spell 335, CT Spell 1130
John 1:1-3: all things came into being through the Logos (echoing Egyptian creation-by-word)
Isis-Ra myth (Papyrus Turin 1993): Ra's secret name contains creative dynamis; name-revelation grants authority
Book of the Dead Spell 17: 'I know the name of that god' grants protection and life
Pyramid Texts Utterance 301: 'I know him, I know his name' secures passage to heaven
John 1:12, 17:6, 17:26: revealing Father's name grants exousia to become children of God
Ra's descent and ascent (Book of the Dead Ch. 15) = Son's descent/ascent (John 3:13, 6:62)
Book of the Dead Spell 17: participatory identification with god's movement
Pyramid Texts Utterance 627: king 'ascends to Re each day' and descends
John 14:3, 17: believers share in Son's return to Father (participatory ascent)
Egyptian judgment: heart weighed against Maat before Osiris (Book of the Dead Spell 125, 30B)
John radicalizes by relocating judgment to the present: belief passes from death to life (John 3:18-19)
Zoe aionios used repeatedly: John 3:16, 5:24, 6:40, 6:54, 11:25-26, 17:3

chapter_inventory.argument: John is not a Jewish text: it contains no covenant narrative, treats Torah as displaced, and abolishes sacred geography.
evidence: No Exodus, Sinai, patriarchs, land promise, or national election narrative anywhere in John
Torah without authority: John 5:39-47 (Scripture searched but doesn't lead to recognition)
Jesus accuses opponents of having law without understanding (John 7:19)
'The Jews' treated as external opposing group (John 8:44, 9:22, 18:36)
Salvation = knowing God and the one he sent (John 17:3), not commandment obedience
Eternal life as present possession (John 5:24), not future resurrection
Sacred geography abolished: John 4:21-24 (neither Jerusalem nor any mountain retains authority)
Moses appears only to be overridden (John 1:17, 5:45-47, 6:32)
Isaiah 53:1 and 6:10 quoted to diagnose rejection, not prove authority (John 12:38-40)
Jewish festivals staged then displaced: Passover (John 2:13, 6:4, 12:1), Tabernacles (John 7:2), Dedication (John 10:22) — each overridden by Jesus's claims
chapter_inventory.section: Why John Is Not a Jewish Text (lines 1003-1024)

chapter_inventory.argument: Matthew is the Judean gospel: dynastic, legal-exegetical, reflecting Herodian Jerusalem environment with Greek-Hebrew bilingual profile.
evidence: Temple Warning Inscription (SEG 8.169): entirely in Greek, proving Greek used for official communication in Jerusalem
Theodotos Synagogue Inscription (CIIP I.2 573): Greek with civic terminology identical to Herodian-era inscriptions
Jerusalem ossuaries (Rahmani catalog): Hebrew and Greek names but no Aramaic formulas
Basileia ton ouranon ('kingdom of the heavens') reproduces Judean avoidance of saying 'God', absent from Greek-speaking regions
Formula-quotations (hina plerothē) imitate Judean exegetical style for legitimizing royal/legal claims
James addresses 'twelve tribes in the diaspora' (James 1:1) as political constituency claim
War Scroll (1QM): final army ordered under banners by twelve tribes
4Q252: interprets Gen 49 as rulership oracle locating authority in Judah
Gen 49:9-10 LXX: skymnos leontos Iouda and ouk ekleipsei archon ex Iouda (lion-Judah-rulership complex)
Revelation 5:5: 'Lion of the tribe of Judah' as royal-messianic title
Sculpted lion head from Herod's Western Palace in Jerusalem (Peleg-Barkat)
Marble lion-head table support from Ramat HaNadiv; marble lion-foot from Cypros near Jericho (Hirschfeld)
Josephus: James tried and executed by Sanhedrin under Ananus (Antiquities 20.200-203)

chapter_inventory.argument: Luke is the Aegean gospel: polished historiographic Greek matching the educational and civic world of the Pauline network cities.
evidence: Luke's preface (Luke 1:1-4): causal participial chain, technical vocabulary (epeideper, parekolouthekoti, akribos), formal patron dedication, promise of orderly narrative (kathexes)
Matches proem structure of Herodotus, Thucydides, Polybius
Honorific kratiste Theophile matches civic decrees from Aegean poleis (IG X.2.1)
Acts 17:6: preserves term politarchai, firmly Macedonian, epigraphically attested at Thessalonica
'We-sections' in Acts employ nautical Greek identical to Aegean voyage literature (Periplus)
Luke's periodic syntax, medical terminology, and civic precision match urban Greek of Macedonia and Asia Minor
Luke tied to Paul: Col 4:14, Phlm 24, 2 Tim 4:11, and 'we' sections in Acts
Pauline corpus attestation: 1 Clement refers to Paul's Corinthian correspondence; Ignatius addresses Pauline cities; Polycarp quotes Paul; P46 attests bound Pauline collection

chapter_inventory.argument: Luke exhibits specifically Greek literary culture: historiographic preface, symposium scenes, divine-son birth pattern, and benefaction proof of authority.
evidence: Key teaching at elite dinner tables matching Greek symposium (Luke 7:36-50, 14:1-24)
Birth narrative: divine paternity announced, child marked as Son of God (Luke 1:35); matches Plutarch's pattern for Alexander and Romulus
Benefaction as primary proof: healing, feeding, restoring life in public view (Luke 7:11-17, 9:10-17)
Acts organized around long public speeches interpreting events (Acts 2, Acts 17), following Greek historian practice
Acts 16:19-26: Paul/Silas arrested, offer no resistance, physical reversal opens doors — echoes Euripides Bacchae 434-518 (Dionysus arrested, prison collapses)

chapter_inventory.argument: Luke is not a Torah text: the Law is placed in the past, Jerusalem is portrayed as rejection site, and the narrative moves decisively away from Israel's institutions.
evidence: Luke 16:16: Law and Prophets lasted until John, then proclamation takes over
Jerusalem as place where prophets are rejected and killed (Luke 13:33-34)
Jesus' death framed as rejection by the city (Luke 19:41-44, Luke 23)
Acts 13:46-47: message moves to Gentile audiences by design
Acts 28:28: salvation redirected to nations rather than restored to Israel
Acts 7:51-53: Israel's leaders portrayed as consistently resistant across generations
Temple as site of judgment: Jesus announces destruction (Luke 19:41-44, 21:5-6); Stephen declares God doesn't dwell in temples made by hands (Acts 7:48-58)
Luke 24:47, Acts 1:8: mission departs Jerusalem permanently
Luke 11:29-32: Jonah comparison — Nineveh repented, Israel rejects; contrast with Matthew 12:40 typological three-days sign
chapter_inventory.section: Why Luke Is Not a Torah Text (lines 1106-1123)

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul and Luke employ Greek cosmology (Platonic-Stoic metaphysics, layered heavens, Greek underworld, ascent logic) rather than Jewish eschatology.
evidence: Paul's soma pneumatikon (1 Cor 15): ascent logic, perishable vs imperishable, earthly vs heavenly — Platonic-Stoic, not Pharisaic
Pneuma as Stoic material substance (fine, active element permeating cosmos), not later metaphysical person of Trinity
Model aligns with heroes translated to Olympus, not Danielic graves opening
Paul's 'third heaven' (2 Cor 12): layered sky cosmology standard in Greek philosophy (Plato, Aristotle, Stoics)
Luke 16:22-23: Lazarus explicitly in Hades, not Sheol, with fixed internal moral geography and uncrossable chasm
Luke 16:22: immediate placement after death rather than deferred judgment
Phil 1:23: death as departure to be with Christ (confirms immediacy)
Abraham placed inside Hades itself (Luke 16:22-25), Jewish figure inside Greek religious system
Luke-Acts: physical ascent into heaven (vertical translation) mirrors Heracles and Romulus apotheosis
chapter_inventory.section: Greek Cosmology in Paul and Luke (lines 1124-1197)

chapter_inventory.argument: Paul's justification by faith (dikaiosyne ek pisteos) is Greek civic-forensic allegiance language, not abstract theology.
evidence: Dikaiousthai ek pisteos = declared in the right on basis of loyalty
Dikaioo denotes status verdict: vindicate, recognize as legitimate, grant standing
Logizomai (reckon, count, credit) paired with dikaioo: righteousness counted, not infused
Pistis in Paul = loyalty, trust, allegiance toward a lord, not belief-content
Christ as kyrios who grants standing; those loyal = dikaiomenoi
Demosthenes uses dikaioo for legal outcomes, not inner virtue
Aristotle defines dikaiosyne as correct placement in ordered system (Nicomachean Ethics 5)
Greek inscriptions use dikaioo for official recognition by assemblies and magistrates
Polybius treats pistis as foundation of political order
Greek treaties bind cities by pistis to hegemonic powers
chapter_inventory.section: Greek Cosmology in Paul and Luke (lines 1157-1197)

chapter_inventory.argument: Mark is the Seleucid gospel, with Petrine origin, Syrian bilingual Greek, and Aramaic traces matching the Antiochene linguistic environment.
evidence: Papias: Mark as Peter's interpreter
1 Peter 1:1 addresses Seleucid frontier provinces: Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, Bithynia
P72 transmits 1-2 Peter with Jude in eastern Catholic collection
Seven Aramaic phrases in Mark: Talitha koum (5:41), Boanerges (3:17), Korban (7:11), Ephphatha (7:34), Rabbouni (10:51), Golgotha (15:22), Eloi Eloi lama sabachthani (15:34)
Phonology and lexicon match western Aramaic of Syrian-Seleucid belt, not Galilee/Judea
Antioch city center: exclusively Greek civic inscriptions (SEG 28.1235, OGIS 256-259, CIJ II 803-809)
Rural Syrian hinterland: Aramaic funerary and dedicatory inscriptions (IGLS IV, CIS II)
Peter writes from Antioch to his mission field (Syria absent from 1 Peter's address because it's his base)
Peter's 'elect exiles of the Dispersion' names Seleucid lands, parallel to James's 'twelve tribes' and Paul's 'sons of men'

chapter_inventory.argument: Mark exhibits Phoenician-Syrian religious patterns: exorcism as primary proof of authority, direct command language matching Baal-type lordship, and territorial obsession.
evidence: First miracle is exorcism in synagogue (Mark 1:21-28); extended possession narratives (Mark 5:1-20, 9:14-29)
More exorcism material proportionally than Matthew or Luke
Jesus commands spirits directly without prayer or appeal (Mark 1:25, 4:39, 9:25), matching West-Semitic lordship where Baal exercises power through direct speech
Baal (lord/master) rendered as ho kyrios in Greek inscriptions from Syria/Phoenicia (CIS II 86, IGLS IV 1264)
Jesus as kyrios in Syrian context = Baal-type lordship
Jesus rebukes wind and waves directly (Mark 4:35-41) — storm-god function
Narrative structured around crossings, borders, regions, foreign zones: Decapolis, Gerasa, Tyre, Sidon (Mark 4-7)
Gerasene demoniac: named spirits, multiple entities, territorial transfer, animal hosts (Mark 5:1-20)
Elijah as rumor identity for Jesus based on power, not prophecy (Mark 6:15, 8:28)
Transfiguration: Elijah as power theophany, not Torah-validating authority (Mark 9:4-13)

chapter_inventory.argument: Mark is not a Torah text: Jesus asserts authority over Sabbath, sets aside purity law, contains no genealogy, and lacks sustained legal teaching.
evidence: Authority over Sabbath without Torah interpretation (Mark 2:23-28, 3:1-6)
Purity law set aside, all foods declared clean (Mark 7:19)
Scripture appears rarely without fulfillment formulas (Mark 1:2-3, 12:10)
No genealogy, no Moses typology, no Sinai echoes, no birth narrative
Temple condemned as corrupt power structure (Mark 11:15-17)
Temple destruction declared inevitable and final (Mark 13:1-2)
Passion narrative lacks covenant theology, sacrificial explanation, or legal reasoning (Mark 14-15)
chapter_inventory.section: Why Mark Is Not a Torah Text (lines 1240-1250)

chapter_inventory.argument: Apparent breaks in the four-religion pattern correspond to passages widely agreed to be non-original or late additions.
evidence: Daniel in Mark 13:14 (abomination of desolation) and Mark 14:62 (Son of Man on clouds) — widely judged late, crisis-driven additions tied to Temple destruction
Luke 21:20-27 copies Daniel material from Mark/Matthew but empties it of Daniel's court theology
2 Peter 2:4 introduces Tartarus — openly pseudonymous, language appears only after regional anchoring collapsed
Isaiah 40:3-5 appears in all four gospels at John the Baptist (Mark 1:2-3, Matt 3:3, Luke 3:4-6, John 1:23) — shared historical witness, not unique construction
Luke 4:25-27: Elijah/Elisha cited for non-Israelite settings to argue against Israel's exclusivity

chapter_inventory.argument: Jesus's four titles correspond to the four cultural poles: Son of God (Egyptian), Son of Man (Aegean), King of the Jews (Judean), Lord/Kyrios (Seleucid).
evidence: Son of God: Egyptian royal title, pharaoh literally son of the god, Ptolemies adopted without dilution
Son of Man: Aegean political title, representative human ruler who judges nations; anthropos as corporate figure in Greek political language; Daniel 7 translates into this framework
King of the Jews: Judean dynastic title, Davidic lineage, Temple-centered, the charge for which Rome executed him

Kyrios (Lord): Seleucid sovereignty term; Antiochus III uses kyrieia (Welles RC 18); cities 'of which he became kyrios' (Polybius 5.87.4); officials act 'kata to prostagma tou kyriou basileos' (P.Tebt. I 5)
Baal deities in Greek as ho kyrios theos (IGLS III 1186)
Hosea 2:16 LXX replaces Baal with Kyrios
Acts 16:9: 'man of Macedonia' (aner Makedon) personifies Aegean Greek world as human counterpart to beastly empires
Eph 3:5: 'sons of men' (tois huiois ton anthropon) refers to Paul's Aegean audience
Acts 2:36: 'God made him both Lord and Christ' — appointment language (Seleucid legitimacy), not birth language
Mark frames messiahship through Psalm 110: 'The Lord said to my Lord' — lordship transfer, not genealogy

chapter_inventory.argument: The evidence shows Jesus did not speak Aramaic: Mark's Aramaic phrases come from the Antiochene linguistic environment, not from Jesus.
evidence: Aramaic phrases in Mark (Talitha koum, Ephphatha, Rabbouni, Eloi Eloi) are imperatives, nicknames, shouted phrases — most porous elements of bilingual speech
Every Aramaic expression matches western Aramaic dialects of Syrian-Seleucid belt, not Galilee
Antioch: Greek civic center with Aramaic-speaking hinterlands producing natural bilingual mixture
Only Mark preserves these phrases; other gospels ignore them
Galilean inscriptional record: early 1st-century inscriptions from Sepphoris, Japhia, Kefar Hananya, Gischala, and coins of Herod Antipas are Greek
No securely dated Aramaic inscription found from any Galilean site
Galilee does not show the bilingual ecology reflected in Mark
chapter_inventory.section: Why the Evidence Shows Jesus Did Not Speak Aramaic (lines 1291-1303)

chapter_inventory.argument: Once each gospel is placed in its proper regional environment, long-standing mysteries across all four texts resolve themselves.
evidence: Matthew: prophecy obsession, fulfillment quotation density, double genealogy through Joseph and Mary = Judean elite dynastic practice; Sermon on the Mount = Judean halakhic discourse in Greek; anti-Pharisee polemic = intra-Judean elite conflict
Luke: census narrative integrates Jesus into bureaucratic categories; tight chronological framework with named officials; interest in Roman officials; universalism matches Aegean polis worldview

John: Logos = Alexandrian Jewish philosophy; long discourses = Alexandrian philosophical dialogues; light/darkness dualisms = Alexandrian Platonized Jewish worldview; high Christology from Alexandrian Judaism
Mark: exorcism focus matches Syrian religious culture; abrupt scene shifts and heroic structure fit Syrian literary taste for Greek popular epic

chapter_inventory.argument: The epistolary map shows each epistle addresses a region matching one of the four imperial poles, with earliest attestation and papyrus evidence supporting the clustering.
evidence: Romans, 1-2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians/Colossians, Philippians, 1-2 Thessalonians, Philemon: Aegean/Pauline pole; attested by 1 Clement, Ignatius, Polycarp; P46
Hebrews: Alexandrian homily; discussed by Clement/Origen; P13, P46
James, Jude: Judea/Jerusalem pole; attested by Origen, Irenaeus
1-2 Peter: Seleucid provinces; attested by Polycarp, Irenaeus; P72
1-3 John: Egyptian pole; attested by Irenaeus, Muratorian Fragment; P9
Marcion (c. 140): Aegean canon (edited Luke + Pauline Apostolikon)
Egypt: strongest early Johannine cluster in papyrus caches

chapter_inventory.argument: Revelation functions as an operational war text and imperial restoration document, not merely apocalyptic consolation.
evidence: Beast = Nero (666 = Hebrew gematria of Neron Kaisar; 616 = Latin variant)
Babylon = Rome (city on seven hills, rules over kings of the earth; Rev 17:9, 17:18)
Four living creatures around God's throne (Rev 4:6-8): legitimate imperial plurality with differentiated powers
Babylon's beast: single hybrid monster absorbing lion, bear, leopard (Rev 13:2) = Rome's totalizing empire
Dragon (Rev 12-13) distinct from Beast: 'gave the Beast his power and his throne' (Rev 13:2) = supra-political principle of universal empire
Dragon represents ideology of Hellenistic divine kingship passing from Persia to Alexander to Diadochi to Rome
Christ appears as basileus with diadems commanding heavenly strateuma
'He shall shepherd the nations with a rod of iron' — sovereignty/governance vocabulary
chapter_inventory.section: Revelation as Imperial Restoration by Revolt from Rome (lines 1388-1423)

chapter_inventory.argument: The seven messages of Revelation 2-3 function as assessments of readiness along the primary Roman communication circuit of Asia province.
evidence: Sequence follows Roman communication circuit: Ephesus (provincial capital) → Smyrna → Pergamum → Thyatira → Sardis → Philadelphia → Laodicea
Route corresponds to main postal, military, commercial axis used by Persian, Hellenistic, and Roman administrations

Each city occupies strategic position: provincial capital (Ephesus), imperial cult center (Smyrna), former Attalid capital (Pergamum), inland garrison (Thyatira), Lydian admin center (Sardis), frontier settlement (Philadelphia), financial hub (Laodicea)
Messages follow inspection report form: knowledge of works, commendation/deficiency, command to correct, consequences for noncompliance
Threats as termination clauses: removal of lampstand, surprise visitation, expulsion
'The one who conquers' (ho nikon) frames sequence in terms of victory/imperial contest
Contrast with Paul: Pauline networks expand across provinces; Johannine circuit is closed loop within single administrative unit (audit vs. mission)

chapter_inventory.argument: Revelation's seals, trumpets, and bowls form a structured operational sequence from inspection to execution of regime change.
evidence: Seals: establish legal authority over succession and judgment, initiate destabilization (famine, violence, death)
Trumpets: mobilization signals and shock operations targeting infrastructure, population, cosmic legitimacy
Bowls: terminal judgments incapacitating regime's capacity to govern and enforce loyalty
Four horsemen = initial destabilization phase
Two witnesses = juridical envoys asserting rival authority; execution and vindication signal loss of mandate
Mark of the Beast (charagma): economic control encoding allegiance to Rome's cultic state
Seal of God (sphragis): counter-administrative system marking subjects of rival sovereignty
Woman clothed with the sun = dynastic continuity; child's removal to God's throne preserves succession
Rider on white horse: sovereign executing judgment with multiple diadems; name Logos tou Theou
New Jerusalem: rival capital with walls, gates, foundations, throne — displaces Rome's urban theology
Kings bringing glory into city presupposes political reorganization, not abolition of history

chapter_text:
With a deeper understanding of the historical Jesus, the interpretation of the events that followed his death can change quite dramatically as well.

In the context of an influential royal figure with a large following and with early texts in circulation, the early Christian movement can be seen in a new light.

If we acknowledge that Jesus was framed in Greek and Egyptian royal cult, then Christianity appears from the very beginning as an extension of that tradition.
Jesus did not emerge in a vacuum with completely new ideas.
He was a continuation of the recently fallen Greek empire and inherited a movement that already spanned a major part of Rome.

In this chapter we will try to view the events of the New Testament after Jesus’ death in the light of what we found in previous chapters.
As the Gospel of Matthew closes: πορευθέντες οὖν μαθητεύσατε πάντα τὰ ἔθνη --- “Go, therefore, and make disciples of all nations.”

\section{Who were the gentiles?}\label{sec:who-were-the-gentiles}

The word “gentiles” has long been misapplied in the context of the New Testament.
It is almost universally taken to mean “anyone who is not Jewish.”
The confusion comes from the Hebrew word \texthebrew{גּוֹיִם} (\textit{goyim}), which in many contexts does carry this meaning.
While in the Septuagint, \textit{ethnē} often is used as a translation of \texthebrew{גּוֹיִם} (\textit{goyim}), it does not show any sign of the same meaning in the context of the New Testament or other Hellenistic works and inscriptions.
The key point missed by most interpreters is that \textit{panta ta ethnē} does not refer to \texthebrew{גּוֹיִם} (\textit{goyim}) or to all peoples universally, but to the nations of the Greek world.
In Hellenistic inscriptions, the phrase “all the nations” (\textit{panta ta ethnē}) is repeatedly used for the \textit{oikoumene}, the civilized world subject to the emperor.
It was imperial language, not global anthropology.
Thus, Matthew’s Great Commission is not an open-ended summons to the Americas or India, but to the Greek-speaking nations of the empire.

Καὶ εὐλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τῆς γῆς --- “And in your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed” (Gen 22:18 LXX).
This verse is quoted in Galatians 3:8 to argue that “all the nations” were meant to be part of God’s covenant.
Here the nations are not portrayed as outsiders, but as peoples folded into God’s promise.

Βασιλεῖς τῆς γῆς καὶ πάντες λαοὶ, ἄρχοντες καὶ πάντες κριταὶ γῆς… ὕμνος πᾶσι τοῖς ὁσίοις αὐτοῦ, τοῖς υἱοῖς Ἰσραήλ, λαῷ ἐγγίζοντι αὐτῷ --- “Kings of the earth and all peoples, rulers and all judges of the earth… a hymn for all His saints, the sons of Israel, a people near to Him” (Ps 148:11, 14 LXX).
The kings and peoples of the earth are included in God’s rule, yet the focus remains on Israel as the center.

The same idiom appears in imperial decrees, for example in the already cited Priene calendar inscription (9 BC), which describes the birthday of Augustus as “gospel for all the nations,” clearly referring to the subjects of the empire rather than to the entire globe.

“All the nations” in these texts does not mean “non-Jews.”
It refers to all peoples under God’s sovereignty, which in a Hellenistic and imperial context meant the nations integrated into the covenantal and political order of the \textit{oikoumene}.
That included Jews as well as Greeks and others within the world of the recently fallen Greek empire.

\section{Are Acts a work of fiction?}\label{sec:are-acts-a-work-of-fiction}

The Acts of the Apostles describes the actions of the apostles after Jesus' ascension through Paul's Roman custody, ending around 60--62 AD.
It follows the travels of Paul and Peter to all the major cities of the former Greek empire, and describes the interactions of the apostles with the church authorities there.
The Acts of the Apostles is often dismissed as a work of fiction.
And indeed, if Acts are read as a history of the spread of a new religion, we already run into major contradictions.
Most notably the churches described in Acts and the epistles appear fully formed, with no indication of how they were established.
There is actually very little in the way of religious conversion in there.
Educated Greek discourse already spoke in ``highest god'' terms, and Acts depicts Paul exploiting this register when he addresses the Athenian elite about the ``unknown god.''
A lot of the speeches in Acts are clearly political speeches, and while there are certainly some religious elements to them, they do not resemble the speeches of a religious missionary.
It is always argued that to be an effective missionary, one must first establish a rapport with the audience, and so describe the local beliefs and then show how the new religion is compatible with them.
And so the definition of God as the omnipotent force behind the universe, who created everything, and who is not confined to a temple, is a perfect fit for the Hellenistic monotheism that was already present in the former Greek empire.
To wit, during the episodes in Ephesus, where there is a strong cult of Artemis, and physical idols, Paul does attack the beliefs directly.
So this looks like praising the modern ideas of Greek faith and denouncing the old ones, not trying to find commonalities between Judaism and the whatever the local beliefs were.

Once we understand more clearly what apostles and churches were, the Acts of the Apostles makes a lot more sense.
For centuries apostles have been emissaries of centralized state and ecclesia is the civic assembly of the city, local government.
These were not religious terms but administrative ones.
We have well-developed councils, bishops, deacons and treasuries.
This makes absolutely no sense if we assume this is a formation of a new church with a handful of members.
It makes a lot of sense if we remember all of this already existed as part of the Greek civic order, and the apostles were simply trying to maintain the existing order under a new reality of Roman rule.
In that light the Acts of the Apostles is a record of trying to maintain the Greek civic order under a new reality of Roman rule.
There are many references to acts of recommendation, letters of introduction, confiding common law between the ecclesia of different cities, and numerous references to newly appointed officials.
After the conquest and the rebellions, the texts were indeed altered to account for a failed rebellion rather that a failed prophecy of the end of the world.

Like we see clear additions in the gospels that are often dated to 70 AD or shortly after, we can suspect that the Acts of the Apostles were written at the time of the edits.
We are looking here at the second generation of Greeks and Jews under direct and harsh Roman rule coming to terms with the reality of failed rebellions.

\subsection{What is quite striking is that Paul and others write to so many different churches over the short period of time.}\label{subsec:what-is-quite-striking-is-that-paul-and-others-write-to-so-many-different-churches-over-the-short-period-of-time.}

There is a major challenge for the traditional timeline of the apostles establishing so many churches in such a short period of time.
These churches would have to all be established, grow, keep up to date with the fastly shifting theology, and then do nearly nothing for the next 100 years.
These churches immediately showed up in every single major city in the former Greek empire, and no churches showed up anywhere else.
All of the correspondence and scripture was written in Greek, and no other languages.
It is important to point out that Greek was absolutely not the lingua franca in any part of the Roman empire that was not recently part of the Greek empire.
The lingua franca of the Roman empire was Latin, and that was the only language that was used in the administration and the primary language used by the authors.
If the apostles were to establish churches everywhere in the Roman empire, and not just in the former Greek empire, then we would have had the epistles to the extremely prominent cities of Mediolanum, Lutetia, Aquilea, Lugdunum, Memphis, and Londinium.
The truth is that no matter how we model the growth of the early church, it is not possible to explain the patterns we observe.

Even more striking is the total absence of Aramaic or Hebrew letters.
If the apostles' mission were Jewish in nature, at least some correspondence would survive in the languages of Judea.
Instead, all the letters are in Greek, to Greek assemblies, proving that the movement was imperial and Hellenistic at its core.
The same institutional pattern running through Acts becomes unmistakable in Paul's own letters, which operate not as private devotions but as imperial circulars to Greek civic assemblies.

\subsection{Paul as Imperial Envoy and Administrator}\label{subsec:pauls-letters-as-state-correspondence.}

Paul's letters read like circular correspondence from a royal chancery, not like private notes to small devotional groups.
He addresses \textit{ekklesiai}, the same civic assemblies that governed Greek cities, and he orders public reading, courier circulation, and inter-city dissemination exactly as administrative circulars were handled.
His greetings from co-workers function like official co-signatories, and his directives on discipline, finance, and adjudication mirror the instructions sent from higher authority to subordinate poleis.
This is the epistolary footprint of an imperial envoy issuing policy, not of a village preacher sending pious advice.

\subsection{Paul's Letters as Executive Correspondence}\label{subsec:pauls-letters-as-executive-correspondence}

Some scholars dismiss portions of the Pauline corpus as forgeries on the basis of stylistic and theological variation.
That judgment rests on a modern literary model that equates authorship with uniform personal prose.
The letters themselves contradict this model at the level of explicit evidence.
Romans 16:22 names Tertius as the physical writer, directly separating authority from penmanship.
Paul's autograph formulas appear as ratification marks, not as claims of total composition.
``I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand'' functions as formal approval of a completed document (1 Cor 16:21, Gal 6:11, Col 4:18, 2 Thess 3:17).
Such authentication is precisely how delegated correspondence was validated in the ancient world.
Greek and Roman elite figures did not draft letters line by line.
Strategoi, envoys, and league officials issued instructions orally and entrusted composition to staff.
Assistants shaped vocabulary, sentence structure, and rhetorical polish within an approved policy framework.
This administrative model predicts stylistic variation across authorized letters.
A single dictator would produce stronger stylistic uniformity than the Pauline corpus exhibits.
Paul's named associates function as operational staff rather than devotional companions.
They carry letters, manage funds, arbitrate disputes, and represent Paul in person.
By ancient standards, a letter drafted by authorized staff fully embodied the sender's identity and authority.
If Paul supplied direction, policy, and approval, the resulting letters were unambiguously Pauline.
The division between ``authentic'' and ``deutero-Pauline'' letters therefore rests on an anachronistic definition of authorship.
No ancient reader would have regarded an authorized Pauline letter as a forgery.

His political profile matches the letters.
He appeals to Caesar as a Roman citizen.
He travels through the network of Greek poleis that once formed the backbone of Alexander's empire.
His address in Athens (Acts 17) is a philosophical-diplomatic speech pitched at elites, not a sectarian sermon.
Acts frames him as the authorized representative of the Christ-emperor to the cities of the Greek world.
Acts also frames Paul through recognizable Greek literary archetypes.
In Acts 16 he and Silas sing in prison until an earthquake opens the doors, a scene that echoes Euripides' \emph{Bacchae} where the imprisoned Dionysus shakes the prison loose, and in Acts 17 he debates in the Areopagus like Socrates before the Athenian elite.
The narrative casts Paul as both ecstatic divine envoy and philosophical sage within the Greek cultural canon.

His vocabulary confirms the administrative register.
He lists \textit{thronoi}, \textit{kyriotētes}, \textit{archai}, and \textit{exousiai} as if reading a court hierarchy (Col 1:16; Eph 1:21).
\textit{Thronoi} are sovereign seats of rule.
\textit{Kyriotētes} are lordships with jurisdictional dominion.
\textit{Archai} are magistracies and command offices.
\textit{Exousiai} are delegated legal powers to act and enforce.
Christ is enthroned ``at the right hand'' above every such office (Eph 1:20--21).

His military language is constitutional, not mystical.
